pectoralis-major.name: Pectoralis Major
pectoralis-major.function: The pectoralis major, commonly known as the chest, is the largest muscle of the anterior chest wall. The chest contains two main heads, the clavicular head (upper chest) and the sternocostal head. The main function of the chest is flexion, adduction, and medial rotation of the arm at the glenohumeral joint (main joint of the shoulder). The clavicular head primarily functions to flex the humerus, which is lifting the arm forward and upward. While the sternocostal head's main function is to adduct, medially rotate, and extend the humerus. The sternocostal head pulls the arms towards the body and rotates the humerus inward.
pectoralis-major.exercises:
It is in your best interest to do two chest exercises, one that biases the clavicular head and one that biases the sternocostal head.

  • Clavicular Head Exercises:
    • Incline smith machine press
    • Incline dumbbell/barbell press
    • Low-to-high cable fly
  • Sternal Head Exercises:
    • Pec-deck Machine
    • Flat smith machine press
    • Flat Dumbbell/Bench Press

Pick one exercise from each list and do 2-3 sets for each exercise based on your preference.

bicep.name: Biceps Brachii
bicep.function:
The biceps brachii, commonly known as the biceps, is a large, thick muscle on the upper arm's ventral portion. The muscle is composed of a short head and a long head. The long head is located on the lateral side of the biceps brachii, while the short head is located on the medial side.

The main functions of the biceps are the flexion and supination of the forearm. The biceps brachii are more advantaged and active when the forearm is supinated (palm facing up) compared to a neutral or pronated grip. The brachialis muscle is the strongest flexor of the elbow in the absence of supination. The brachialis muscle is located deep to the biceps brachii.
bicep.exercises:
Since we are trying to target the biceps brachii, rather than the brachioradialis muscle, any supinated curl will work.

  • Preacher curls
  • Barbell curls
  • Dumbbell curls
  • Cable curls

Notice that there aren't any curl variations that target the brachialis muscle. This is because the brachialis becomes advantaged when the forearm is neutral or pronated, but this disadvantages the biceps brachii. Thus we will talk about it in the forearm section alongside the brachioradialis muscle.


Pick one exercise and do 2-3 sets based on your preference.

tricep.name: Triceps Brachii
tricep.function: The triceps, scientifically known as the triceps brachii, is a large muscle located on the back (posterior) of the upper arm. It has three heads: the long head, lateral head, and medial head, which together form the horseshoe shape often seen on the back of the arm. The primary function of the triceps is extension of the elbow joint, meaning it straightens the arm. The long head also assists with shoulder extension and adduction because it attaches to the scapula, while the lateral and medial heads mainly contribute to powerful forearm extension at the elbow.
tricep.exercises:
You should generally include one exercise that biases the lateral and medial heads, and another that emphasizes the long head of the triceps. Ideally, you should have one movement where your humerus is fixed to bias the long head and one where the humerus moves to bias the lateral and medial heads.

  • Lateral and medial bias:
    • JM press
    • Dips
  • Long head bias:
    • Single arm tricep extension
    • Tricep pushdown

Note that is it in your best interest to keep your arm tucked to your body when doing the single arm cable extension.

deltoid.name: Deltoids
deltoid.function: The deltoid is a large muscle that covers the shoulder joint. The deltoid consists of three main components, the anterior (front), lateral (side), and posterior (rear). The main function of the deltoid is shoulder abduction, flexion, and extension. The anterior head is responsible for flexion (raising the arm forward) and medial rotation. The lateral head is responsible for abduction, which is lifting the arm to the side. Finally, the posterior head is responsible for extension (moving the arm backward) and lateral rotation.
deltoid.exercises:
The exercises are divided by which head of the deltoid is being biased.

  • Anterior head:
    • Machine shoulder press
    • Smith machine shoulder press
    • Dumbbell shoulder press
    • Overhead press
  • Lateral head:
    • Cable lateral raise
    • Dumbbell lateral raise
  • Posterior head:
    • Machine reverse pec deck flyes
    • Cable cross-body rear delt flyes
    • Face pulls

It's important to know that the posterior/rear delt is going to be worked during back exercises as well. A heavy row with your elbows flared will sufficiently hit the rear delts.

forearm.name: Forearms
forearm.function:
There are 20 muscles in the forearm that help move your arms, hands, and fingers. There are two maincategories of the forearm muscles: intrinsic and extrinsic. Intrinsic muscles help move your forearms by turning your bones inward and outward, while extrinsic muscles help bend and extend your fingers and thumbs.

Alongside this, your forearm muscles are within two compartments: anterior and posterior. The most prominent muscles are located in the posterior, in the superficial layer. These include the brachioradialis, extensor carpi radialis longus, and flexor carpi ulnaris and radialis. The function of brachioradialis is to flex the elbow when the arm is in a neutral or pronated grip.
forearm.exercises:
The smaller muscles in the forearm will always be indirectly trained when you grip something. Thus we will focus on the brachioradialis muscle. Since the biceps brachii become disadvantaged when the forearm is in a neutral or pronated position, it is best to do any curl in a neutral/pronated grip. Recall from the biceps page that the brachialis is advantaged in a neutral or pronated grip. Therefore any exercise that trains the brachioradialis also trains the brachialis, so it is best to pair one of the following curls with a supinated curl when training the biceps.

  • Hammer curls
  • Reverse barbell curls
  • Reverse cable curls

Pick any exercise that you find most comfortable and do it for 2-3 sets based on preference.


Now if you wanted to train the forearms specifically, it would be in your best interest to do exercises that flex/extend the wrist.

  • Wrist curls
  • Wrist extensions

rectus-abdominis.name: Rectus Abdominis
rectus-abdominis.function: The rectus abdominis is what everyone knows as the "six-pack". It is the most visible portion of the abs. The rectus abdominis consists of two flat parallel muscles separated by connective tissue that extends along the whole length of both sides of the abdomen. The function of the rectus abdominis is to flex the spinal column and tense the anterior abdominal wall and assist in compressing the abdominal contents. It also plays a big role in core stability. The strength of the abdominal muscles is important for your health and everyday life.
rectus-abdominis.exercises:
Don't fall for those 30 minute circuits for a "six-pack". The rectus abdominis are a muscle just like any other, so why train them differently? Any good spinal flexion exercises will train the rectus abdominis.

  • Spinal Flexion Exercises:
    • Cable Crunches
    • Crunch Machine
    • Weighted Decline Crunches

Pick one exercise from the list and do 2-3 sets based on your preference.

obliques.name: Obliques
obliques.function:
The obliques are abdominal muscles that function in conjunction with the rectus abdominis to form the core musculature. The obliques comprise of two primary components: the external obliques and the internal obliques. The external obliques are the most superficial abdominal muscles, extending from the lower ribs to the pelvis. These muscles cover the lateral aspects of the abdominal region, while the internal obliques lie deep to the external obliques on each side of the trunk.

The primary functions of the external obliques include facilitating trunk rotation, contributing to trunk flexion through bilateral contraction in coordination with the rectus abdominis and internal obliques by drawing the pubis toward the xiphoid process (as observed in movements such as crunches or sit-ups), stabilizing the core, compressing the abdominal cavity, assisting with forced expiration during respiration, and enabling lateral flexion of the trunk.
obliques.exercises:
Since a primary function of the obliques are trunk rotation and lateral flexion, any exercise that involves trunk rotation or lateral flexion will train the obliques.

  • Cable Woodchoppers
  • Hanging Knee Raises

Pick one exercise and do 2-3 sets based on your preference.

trapezius.name: Trapezius
trapezius.function:
The trapezius is a broad, superficial muscle on the posterior neck and trunk, divided into three parts: descending (superior), ascending (inferior), and middle. It primarily supports posture by maintaining spinal alignment and coordinates scapular movements with several muscles.

The rhomboids (major and minor) assist in scapular retraction and stabilization. The levator scapulae elevates and rotates the scapula, working with the upper trapezius to lift the shoulder and aid in downward rotation. The serratus anterior protracts and stabilizes the scapula against the rib cage, collaborating with the lower trapezius for upward scapular rotation during arm elevation. The latissimus dorsi partners with the lower trapezius in scapular downward rotation and facilitates shoulder adduction, extension, internal rotation, and movements such as pulling, rowing, and swimming. The upper fibres of the trapezius elevate the scapula and extend the neck. The middle fibres retract the scapula and the lower fibres depress the scapula.
trapezius.exercises:
The upper trap receives sufficient stimulus from heavy compound back exercises. Therefore any exercise where the scapula is being retracted will suffice.

  • Wide-grip machine rows
  • Wide-grip seated cable rows
  • T-bar Rows
  • Kelso Shrugs
  • Shrugs

Ensure your elbows are more flared for the rows. Anything with a chest support will be more stable. Notice I put shrugs in, incase you did want to do isolation work for the upper traps.


Pick one exercise from the list (excluding shrugs) and do 2-3 sets based on your preference.

latissimus-dorsi.name: Latissimus Dorsi
latissimus-dorsi.function:
The latissimus dorsi, or lats, is a broad, flat muscle covering most of the posterior thorax. Its primary function involves upper extremity movement. As a scapular motion muscle, it pulls the scapula's inferior angle in various directions, facilitating shoulder joint movements such as internal rotation, adduction, and arm extension.

Although a single large muscle, the lats can be functionally divided into upper and lower regions based on fiber orientation: the upper fibers run more horizontally, and the lower fibers more vertically. This distinction suggests a functional bias in training focus, despite limited scientific evidence supporting this approach.
latissimus-dorsi.exercises:
It is best to have an exercise where you adduct your shoulders (bring your arms towards your body) and extend your shoulders (bring your arms behind your body). To do so, you should have an exercise where you pull in the frontal plane and one where you pull in the saggital plane.

  • Frontal plane:
    • Wide-grip lat pulldowns
    • Wide-grip pullups
    • Wide-grip seated cable rows
  • Sagittal plane:
    • Close-grip lat pulldowns
    • Close-grip pullups
    • Close-grip seated cable rows
    • Close-grip machine rows

Pick one exercise for the frontal plane and the saggital plane and perform 2-3 sets per exercise based on your preference.

erector-spinae.name: Erector Spinae
erector-spinae.function: The erector spinae is a large, superficial muscle located just beneath the thoracolumbar fascia, originating from the erector spinae aponeurosis. It consists of the iliocostalis, longissimus, and spinalis muscles. Its primary function is to extend the vertebral column through bilateral contraction, straightening the back. Additionally, it enables lateral flexion, supports upright posture, and controls forward bending. Together, these muscles straighten the spine from a flexed position and facilitate backward bending.
erector-spinae.exercises:
The erector spinae muscles are primarily trained through exercises that involve extending the vertebral column. Thus, any hip hinge movement will train the erector spinae.

  • Back extensions
  • Romanian deadlifts
  • Straight leg deadlifts
  • Deadlifts
  • Good mornings
  • Reverse hyperextensions

You may notice that some of these exercises primarily train your hamstrings and glutes as well. Therefore, you should do your hip hinge movement on your leg/lower days instead of back.


Pick one exercise and do 2-3 sets based on your preference.

glutes.name: Glutes
glutes.function: The glutes, or gluteal muscles, are a group of three muscles that form the buttocks: the gluteus maximus, gluteus medius, and gluteus minimus. The main functions of the gluteal muscles include hip extension, lateral (external) rotation of the hip, hip abduction, and stabilization of the pelvis, while also helping to maintain an erect posture. They play an important role in lower-body movements such as walking, running, climbing, and standing up from a bent position.
glutes.exercises:
The glutes should be hit in a contracted and stretched position.

  • Stretched:
    • Romanian deadlifts
    • Bulgarian split squats
  • Contracted:
    • Hip thrusts
    • Glute bridges
    • Cable kickbacks

Pick one exercise from each list and do 2-3 sets per exercise based on preference.

hamstrings.name: Hamstrings
hamstrings.function: The hamstrings comprise three muscles: the semitendinosus, semimembranosus, and biceps femoris, primarily responsible for knee flexion. The semimembranosus and semitendinosus facilitate hip extension, knee flexion, and internal rotation of the lower leg when the knee is flexed. The biceps femoris contributes to hip extension, knee flexion, and external rotation of the lower leg during slight knee flexion, and aids in external rotation of the thigh when the hip is extended. Collectively, the hamstrings play a crucial role in knee movement and locomotion.
hamstrings.exercises:
Since the hamstrings are responsible for knee flexion and hip extension, you should perform one exercise with knee flexion and one for hip extension.

  • Knee flexion:
    • Seated leg curls
    • Lying leg curls
    • Nordic curls
    • Standing leg curls
  • Hip extension:
    • Romanian deadlifts
    • Straight leg deadlifts
    • Deadlifts

Pick one exercise for knee flexion and one for hip extension and do 2-3 sets for each exercise based on your preference.

quadriceps-femoris.name: Quadriceps Femoris
quadriceps-femoris.function: The quadriceps femoris is the most voluminous muscle of the body. It is a hip flexor and knee extensor that consists of four individual muscles. The four muscles are the rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, and vastus intermedius. They form the main bulk of the thigh and collectively are one of the most powerful muscles in the body. The quadriceps all work together to extend the knee, with the rectus femoris also flexing the hip and the vastus medialis adducting the thighs. The quadriceps femoris are used every day when you walk, jump, climb stairs, etc.
quadriceps-femoris.exercises:
The main functions of the quadriceps femoris are to extend the knee and flex the hip. Thus a pressing pattern and a extension pattern will cover all the muscles of the quadriceps femoris. A pressing pattern will primarily target the vastus lateralis and vastus medialis and a extension will help target the rectus femoris, which often gets less activation during a pressing pattern.

  • Pressing patterns:
    • Leg press
    • Barbell squat
    • Smith machine squat
    • Pendulum squat
    • Hack squat
  • Leg extension

Pick one exercise from the pressing patterns and do a leg extension for 2-3 sets each based on preference.

calf.name: Calf
calf.function: The calf consists of the gastrocnemius, soleus and the plantaris muscles in the posterior lower leg. These muscles work together for plantarflexion (pushing toes down) and, along with the achilles tendon are essential for propelling the body. The gastrocnemius is the large, superficial muscle of the calf, which is often what people think when they refer to the calf. The soleus is a deep muscle located under the gastrocnemius that is primary for plantarflexion and postural stability.
calf.exercises:
The gastrocnemius is best targeted with exercises that involve plantarflexion with the knee extended. The soleus is best targeted with exercises that involve plantarflexion with the knee flexed. The gastrocnemius is in a state of active insufficiency when your knees are bent; thus, it is in your best interest to do calf raises with a straight leg rather than seated.

  • Straight leg calf raise machine
  • Leg press calf raise

Pick a exercise and perform 2-3 sets based on your preference.
